text: The surgeon marks the incision site on the abdomen before proceeding with a vertical incision for the laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
The surgeon uses a scalpel to make an incision down to the fascia, then employs S retractors to sweep away the subcutaneous fat from the fascia at the midline.

The surgeon demonstrates careful handling of the scalpel blade to avoid catching on the skin while making an incision. The fascia is visible, and the surgeon cuts it under direct vision, rarely using Coker clamps.
The surgeon uses two hemostats to grasp the peritoneum, which appears very thin, and then uses an S-retractor to sweep it open without making a sharp incision.

The surgeon places two stay sutures from side to side for closure, using an S-retractor to expose the fascia. The first stitch is placed across the fascial opening, and a hemostat is used to hold the suture tails together.

The surgeon places an S retractor into the fascial opening to expose the fascia, followed by the placement of a second stitch across the fascia. The S retractor is repositioned to maintain exposure, and additional sutures are placed as needed.

The surgeon places an additional retractor inside the abdominal cavity, using it as a shoe horn. The Hassan Trocar's collar is adjusted to the appropriate length and locked in place. The collar is secured with stay sutures, and insufflation tubing is connected.
The surgeon places the epigastric port high, just below the lower edge, directing it towards the cystic duct. The port is positioned slightly more laterally to avoid the falciform ligament.

The surgeon demonstrates the placement of a trocar, ensuring it is directed laterally away from the surgeon to prevent the trocars from being positioned too closely together inside the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon places the lateral trocar in a skive fashion, with the tip moving from a posterior to an anterior trajectory. The angle is demonstrated for the five-in-one trocar to optimize the positioning of the fundus grasper, although the angle achieved is slightly off from the intended position.

The surgeon uses a five-millimeter, thirty-degree scope to view the surgical field. The gallbladder is retracted by lifting its fundus and handing it to the grasper inserted through the lateral trocar. The angle of the grasper is demonstrated to avoid puncturing the gallbladder as the tips of the instrument dig into it during lifting.

The surgeon adjusts the grip on the gallbladder, revealing a small capsule or tear on the liver due to an adhesion, which is cauterized. The surgeon then lifts the gallbladder to assess adhesions around its neck and uses the left hand to manipulate the periduodenal fat, freeing the duodenum from the gallbladder.

The surgeon applies traction to the gallbladder and duodenum to remove adhesions, freeing the gallbladder neck. The gallbladder is manipulated back and forth to find a suitable plane for dissection, further releasing the duodenum from the gallbladder. Additional retraction is applied to improve exposure of the gallbladder.

The surgeon demonstrates the direction of retraction needed for the cystic duct, aligning the instrument in line with the duct. The instrument is positioned closer to the rib cage to optimize the retraction angle, ensuring proper exposure of the surgical field.

The surgeon visualizes the cystic artery and begins clearing fat from the gallbladder neck to better expose the cystic duct and artery. The gallbladder is manipulated back and forth to assess the optimal side for dissection, ultimately focusing on the lateral aspect to carefully free the lateral peritoneal attachments.

The surgeon uses a hook to dissect into the proper plane, moving it back and forth to divide tissues. Small vessels are carefully crossed with the hook to ensure complete division. With the gallbladder now freed, the surgeon works on the medial aspect, freeing peritoneal attachments using the hook for precise dissection.

The surgeon clears fat and tissues anterior and posterior to the cystic duct using a hook, pushing the cystic artery to identify the dissection plane. The cystic artery is mobilized off the gallbladder wall, exposing the backside of the cystic duct and the undersurface of the gallbladder.

The surgeon uses a hook to create a window behind the cystic artery, pulling back connective tissue to cauterize and divide it. The view behind the gallbladder neck is partially visible, and the surgeon attempts to develop a window underneath the gallbladder, adjusting for better exposure.

The surgeon divides additional attachments on the backside of the gallbladder and flips it back to the medial aspect. Using a hook, the surgeon enlarges the window behind the gallbladder, then employs Grasper jaws to further expose and carefully enlarge the window. The anatomy is clearly defined, though the cystic plate is not entirely cleared for a critical view of safety.

The surgeon continues to free the gallbladder by dividing lateral and medial peritoneal attachments using hook cautery. The cystic duct appears slightly prominent as the surgeon works on the medial aspect to achieve a critical view of safety. During the process, a small opening is made into the gallbladder, resulting in bile drainage.

The surgeon uses a hook to carefully free the gallbladder from the liver, aided by the decompression of the gallbladder. A 5mm clip applier is used to place one clip on the cystic artery and another on the gallbladder neck. The suction irrigator is connected to remove spilled bile from the gallbladder.

The surgeon performs a cystic ductotomy using scissors, resulting in the return of bile. The cystic duct is milked to check for stones, followed by the insertion of a five French open-ended ureteral catheter secured with a clangogram clamp. The intraoperative clangogram shows normal opacification of the biliary tree without filling defects, and normal contrast runoff into the duodenum.

The surgeon secures the cystic duct with three clips, ensuring they are placed perpendicular to the duct for optimal closure. Two additional clips are placed on the cystic artery, although they are not as well aligned as those on the cystic duct.
The surgeon cuts the cystic duct and cystic artery, then proceeds to free the gallbladder from the gallbladder fossa. The left hand is used to pull the gallbladder away from the liver.

The surgeon works on the lateral aspect of the gallbladder, using the left hand to provide better retraction by pulling the gallbladder laterally. The surgeon then switches to working underneath the gallbladder, using the left hand to grab and release it for improved traction and exposure.

The surgeon uses the heel of the hook to dissect within the gallbladder fossa, identifying a small vessel. The gallbladder is repositioned with the fundus grasper on the true fundus, and lateral attachments are freed. The surgeon then moves to the medial aspect to release peritoneal attachments for better exposure of the vessel.

The surgeon places the left hand underneath the gallbladder to improve exposure and frees up attachments to better visualize a posterior vessel. Using a hook cautery, the surgeon carefully dissects around the vessel, creating a window to ensure there is no biliary communication.

The surgeon uses a clip applier to secure a small vessel close to the liver, avoiding cauterization to prevent dislodging the clip. The heel of the instrument is used to prevent energy conduction to the clip. The surgeon then frees the remaining attachments holding the gallbladder and flips it over the liver.

The surgeon inspects the gallbladder fossa, confirming it is hemostatic with no visible bile leakage. Bile present in the area is irrigated out using a suction irrigator, ensuring clips are not caught in the side holes. The surgeon uses hook cautery to touch two areas before completely releasing the gallbladder from the fossa.

The surgeon moves the laparoscopic camera to the epigastric trocar and places the gallbladder into a specimen bag. Using a suction irrigator, the surgeon cleans the abdominal cavity with small amounts of fluid to dilute and remove bile.
The surgeon takes a final visual inspection of the gallbladder fossa, ensuring no complications are present, before removing the upper trocars under direct vision, concluding the surgical procedure.
